observations.recommendation:
1.3. We recommended and Management agreed that the:
(a) Municipal Treasurer (i) furnish the updated RPTAR/ Taxpayer’s index card to
Municipal Accountant at every beginning of the year for recording and adjustment
purposes; and (ii) intensify and strategize tax collection efforts from taxpayers such
as deployment of demand letters, posting of notice of delinquency in conspicuous
places and tapping the assistance of the barangay officials for tax campaign to reach
out to a larger area; and
(b) Municipal Accountant (i) establish RPT and SET receivables sourced from the list
duly certified by the Treasury Office at the beginning of the year; (ii) exclude those
who made advance payments during the previous year in the total amount to be
established as RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year; (iii) monitor the
unearned revenue for collections from advance payments in the previous year and
make necessary adjustments to recognize the realized/earned revenue at the
beginning of the subsequent year; and (iv) promptly make the necessary adjustments
as soon as possible whenever a misstatement is discovered.

2.22 We recommended and Management agreed that the Accounting Office and
the Supply Officer, as the case may be: (i) ensure compliance with the provisions of
GAM for LGUs by maintaining complete and regularly updated SCs and SLCs based
on actual transactions supported by valid source documents and observe the periodic
reconciliation of the two records to monitor agency’s assets properly; (ii) maintain a
Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to serve as a basis for preparing
journal entries to record the supplies and materials issued; (iii) ensure that all WMRs
are properly and completely filled out, with all required fields duly accomplished and
the signatures of all authorized officials obtained to establish transparency and
accountability regarding the legitimacy of replacements and disposal of waste
materials; and (iv) conform with the implementing guidelines of COA Circular 2022-
004 by ensuring the proper accomplishment and maintenance of all required forms
related to semi-expendable properties. In addition, an organized ICS filing system
should be established and maintained to facilitate efficient monitoring, retrieval, and
verification of custodianship.

3.29 We recommended and Management agreed that the Inventory Committee,
Accounting Office and the Property Officer, as the case may be,: (i) conduct a
complete annual physical count of PPEs and submit the corresponding RPCPPE in
accordance with Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs; (ii) maintain the Property Cards
and PPE Ledger Cards for all LGUs properties; (iii) observe the periodic
reconciliation of the two records to monitor agency’s assets properly; (iv) maintain
an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to properly document
and track the unserviceable assets of the Municipality; (v) comply to the provisions of
Section 259 of MNGAS by ensuring the issuance of a New PAR whenever there is a
change in the custodian or once every three (3) years, as stipulated; (vi) implement a
systematic filing and monitoring system to ensure that all PARs are complete, up-
to-date, and easily accessible; (vii) implement the One-Time Cleansing of the PPE
account balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 to address
discrepancies between recorded balances and actual physical assets; and (viii)
conduct regular reconciliations between the GL and PPELC, ensuring that all PPE
transactions are consistently and accurately recorded in both records.

4.9 We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant (i)
prepare and maintain SLs per creditor as necessary to support the balances presented
in the GL and financial statements; and (ii) perform a thorough reconciliation of the
payable accounts totaling ₱24,720,823.29 as of December 31, 2024; (iii) exhaust all
remedies to gather the details and supporting documents needed for the identification
of the amounts of dormant and long-outstanding payables to facilitate the reversion
and/or write-off of such in accordance with the provisions of COA-DBM JC No. 1, s.
2021 and COA Circular No. 2016-005; and (iv) assign a qualified personnel to update
and monitor the SLs regularly, ensuring all transactions are recorded timely and
accurately.

5.6 We recommended and Management agreed that the Accounting Section (i)
ensure that all material and relevant information pertaining to the Payables account
is clearly and adequately disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statement, in
compliance with the requirements of IPSAS 1; (ii) provide details on the efforts made
to reconcile these accounts, including any limitations or constraints faced; and (iii)
explain the potential financial impact or risks associated with these balances,
including any provisions or allowances made, if any.

6.19 We recommended and Management agreed to: (i) immediately review and
align its accounting procedures with the provisions of COA Circular 2024-009 to
ensure the proper recognition, measurement, and reporting of employee benefits in
accordance with IPSAS 39-Employee Benefits; (ii) adopt the prescribed accounting
entries for the initial set-up, accrual, and settlement of Terminal Leave Benefits, as
illustrated in the Circular; (iii) ensure that all transactions are supported by
appropriate documentation to substantiate the balances recorded in the Terminal
Leave Payable and Terminal Leave Benefits accounts; (iv) conduct a thorough
reassessment and recalculation of the terminal leave balances using the proper
methodology outlined in the circular to correct any discrepancies; (v) conduct regular
reconciliation between the HRO’s leave records and the balances recorded in
accounting books to validate the completeness and accuracy of the reported Terminal
Leave Payable and Benefits; (vi) establish and implement a systematic process to
ensure that employee leave records are up-to-date, regularly monitored, and properly
reconciled to ensure that leave balances are accurate, complete, and reliable; (vii)
ensure that all required fields in the Leave Application forms are duly filled-out; and
(viii) develop a centralized and systematic filing system to facilitate the efficient
management and retrieval of these documents.

7.8 We recommended and Management agreed that the (i) process owners of the
Local Roads Asset Management System based on COA Circular 2015-008 to be
mindful of their duties laid down in the said Circular; (ii) Supply/Property Officer
come up with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road Network
Property Card for all roads under the Agency’s jurisdiction; and (iii) Accounting
Office disclose information on Road Networks in the Notes to the FS in accordance
with COA Circular 2015-008.

8.10 We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Local Chief Executive
continue to (i) prioritize projects that promote inclusive growth and sustainable
development within barangay communities on an ongoing basis and (ii) ensure the
active and continuous engagement of barangay stakeholders in identifying and
selecting socio-economic projects to be funded and implemented through the
Development Fund (DF); (b) BAC/TWG (i) establish a checklist and verification
protocol to ensure that advertisements are genuinely posted in the required three
conspicuous places; (ii) develop a formalized system for sending and recording
invitations to observers, including delivery receipts or acknowledgments; (iii)
require recipients to acknowledge receipt of Notice of Award (NOA) and Notice to
Proceed (NTP) formally, and make a follow-up procedure to confirm receipt, as
necessary; (iv) define and enforce strict deadlines for posting NOA on PhilGEPS as
per procurement laws and guidelines; (v) provide bidders with detailed checklists of
required documents to ensure completeness and highlight the requirements during
pre-bid meetings; (vi) capacitate the BAC/TWG including the procurement and
technical staff to thoroughly review and verify completeness of submissions of bids;
and (c) Municipal Accounting and Treasurer to ensure completeness of documentary
requirements before releasing any payment due to the contractors.

9.10 We recommended and Management agreed that the (i) Municipal Engineer
ensure that project contractors observe the proper size and details to be provided on
project signboards including the update thereof to reflect the actual project status in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013; (ii) LCE start an
inquiry about the noted signboard discrepancies and enforce a written warning and/or
sanction against officials or contractors who violated COA Circular No. 2013-004, if
warranted; and (iii) require refund for the cost component of the project on
signboards/tarpaulins that were not produced.

10.14 We recommended and Management agreed that the: a) Municipal Accountant
continue to (i) make necessary corrections/adjustments in affected inventory and
expense accounts; (ii) observe the perpetual method in recognizing inventory and avoid
recording outright expense when accounting for inventoriable goods and DRRM
supplies; (iii) utilize the LDRRMF only for expenses projects that are directly related
to disaster risk reduction and preparation/prevention in accordance with NDRRMC-
DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; and (iv) present the minimum
required disclosure in the Notes to the FS especially if there are other funds received
apart from the regular LDRRMF as specified in COA Circular No. 2014-002; and b)
Supply Officer, in coordination with concerned offices, maintain Stock Cards and
prepare and submit the RIS, SSMI and Inventory List of Items for DRRM to the
Municipal Accountant for recording and adjusting related expense and inventory
accounts.

11.8 We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) members of the
BAC/TWG (i) adhere to the provisions of the Revised IRR as to the completeness of
documents in executing government contracts from requisition, bidding up to award;
(ii) submit the lacking documents presented from the table; (iii) exert extra effort in
doing market surveys by requesting/canvassing quotations from suppliers and make
sure that the quotations received are reasonable and within industry average; (iv)
thoroughly study the items to be procured and make price referencing/benchmarking
with the prices from DTI and other related/relevant/reputable stores so as to be not
limited/restricted by the prices given by the usual suppliers of the LGU; (b) Municipal
Accountant ensure the completeness of documents before effecting payments. We also
recommend that Management implement additional economical/frugal measures by
procuring items individually and personally assembling them into hygiene and safety
kits instead of buying off-the-shelf assembled kits.

12.5 We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) MSWMB (i) prioritize
the review/update of the current SWMP or to expedite the formulation of a new
SWMP to ensure its sustainability, viability and effectiveness; (ii) prepare Long-term
Action Plan and Annual Work Plan and Budget showing the SWM activities to be
implemented and the financial counterpart for each SWM activity; and (iii) continue
to improve the work of the monitoring and evaluation team and require them to
prepare Progress Reports in line with the Annual Work Plan and the SWM Plan; (b)
Management (i) instruct the Municipal Engineer to expedite the installation of water
system at the MRF; (ii) provide the necessary support to the MENRO especially to
enhance the existing operation by properly assigning the JOs directly under the
MENRO for better supervision and smoother operation; (iii) implement additional
methods of processing waste materials in the MRF to further reduce residual wastes;
and (iv) initiate discussions with TARELCO to assess the power capacity and explore
options for upgrading the power supply infrastructure to support the large-capacity
composter’s demand.

13.9. We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) LCE continuously and
constantly monitor, intensify and strategize collection efforts from taxpayers such as
conducting Tax Campaign and sending payment advisory or notice to taxpayers
before the payment period or deadlines and the like; (b) instruct the MTO to (i)
request from the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the list of all BIR registered
business establishments in Mayantoc, Tarlac with their declared gross sales; and (ii)
conduct the examination of books of accounts and pertinent records in accordance
with Section 171 of the LGC, especially businesses/taxpayers who failed to submit
their ITR; (c) instruct the BPLO to remind the businesses/taxpayers on the
submission of certified photocopies of their ITR; and (d) instruct the MAO to
continue (i) record each collections to the proper accounts; and (ii) reconcile with the
MTO whenever a discrepancy or inconsistency is discovered in the recording of
collections.

14.9. We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Local Chief
Executive continue to capacitate the GFPS and concerned officials with the
mechanisms and processes in mainstreaming GAD perspective in the LGU by being
well-informed and updated of GAD-related issuances and/or attending training,
conferences, symposia administered by the PCW, DILG, or other NGAs directly
involved in GAD concerns; (b) GFPS (i) continuously update its GAD database;
(ii) enhance the GPB and AR by completely filling out the needed qualitative and
quantitative aspects of the activities implemented including data on sex-
disaggregation pursuant to MCW, as necessary; and (iii) administer the use of GeRL
assessment tool in order to measure the LGU’s level of gender-responsiveness as an
organization and in the delivery of its basic services; and (iv) utilize the HGDG tool
and PIMME, as necessary, whenever the LGU plans to attribute a portion or the
whole of the program’s budget to the GAD budget supporting gender-responsive
PPAs.

15.9 We recommended and Management agreed that the (a) Officers and
employees in charge of the implementation of projects funded by other agencies to be
mindful of prompt implementation of projects based on the terms and conditions of
the Source Agencies to ensure efficient delivery of public service to the intended
beneficiaries; (b) Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer (i) continue timely
updating the records of all fund transfers from different agencies for proper
monitoring and to establish the reliability of the year-end balance of the accounts;
and (c) MPDO conduct a thorough review to identify the reasons for the non-
utilization of funds in CY 2024 and determine whether the delays were due to
planning inefficiencies, procurement issues, or other administrative bottlenecks.

16.20 We recommended and Management agreed that the LSB chaired by the Local
Chief Executive (LCE) (i) install a feedback mechanism and furnish copy of the
annual SEF Budget to the Local Accountant, Treasurer and Budget Officer for them
to be guided as basis for the disbursement or certification of availability of funds and
for recording purposes; (ii) improve collaboration and abide with the budget
formulation process outlined by DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017 with
complete documentation of comprehensive list of requests from each school
sourced from SIPs; (iii) replace the old forms and present the SEF Budget by
program, activity and project (P/A/P), by allotment class and per school using SEF
Budget Preparation Form No. 1 (Annex A) of the Joint Circular; (iv) ensure that the
approval of the SEF Budget shall be through an LSB Resolution, signed by the Chair
and Co-Chair and majority of all its members, pursuant to Section 100 (b) of RA No.
7160 by adopting a standardized template for the SEF Budget Resolution, clearly
stating the required signatories; (v) disseminate the guidelines, train staff and improve
the process in order to provide the details of the utilization report as prescribed by
Accountability Forms outlined in Joint Circular No. 1; and vi) ensure that SEF
utilization information is displayed in three conspicuous public places as mandated
by the Joint Circular which could include locations such as community centers,
libraries, public market and schools, in addition to the Municipal Hall's Disclosure
Board.

17.11 We recommended and Management agreed that the Budget and Accounting
Section together with the MPDO (i) ensure all planned donations, programs, and projects
are thoroughly identified and documented before the budget preparation. This includes
obtaining detailed work and financial plans from all departments; (ii) clearly specify the
objects of expenditure related to donations in the budget to avoid under-allocation; (iii)
improve coordination between the Office of the Mayor and other departments to
consolidate all donation-related activities into the annual budget; and (iii) implement
the burial assistance program by directly giving the niche to the recipient, if they are
entitled based on parameters/qualifications set by the LGU.

19.6 We recommended and the Management agreed to (i) further enhance fiscal
responsibility and transparency; (ii) always reconcile and provide details on the
difference between taxes withheld and remitted; (iii) strengthen internal controls and
staff training on tax procedures, and adopt systems for accurate tax computation and
timely remittance.

20.6 We recommended, and the LCE has agreed to implement the following
measures to ensure proper financial management and compliance with regulatory
requirements to: (i) continue the prompt and complete remittance of loan
amortizations as well as mandatory contributions to government agencies such as the
Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), Pag-IBIG Fund, and PhilHealth.
These payments should be made within the prescribed deadlines to avoid penalties or
lapses in coverage, thereby safeguarding the benefits and entitlements of all eligible
employees; (ii) exert additional and diligent effort in the collection and review of all
pertinent documents related to the subject accounts. This includes conducting a
thorough analysis and reconciliation of the inter-agency payables to establish and
verify the accuracy and legitimacy of the recorded balances; and
(iii) ensure that all due premiums are remitted immediately upon confirmation and
when sufficient evidence supports the obligation.

21.4 We recommended and the LCE concurred that the Municipal Accountant and
other concerned personnel enforce the immediate settlement of disallowances in
accordance with Section 9.4 of the 2009 RRSA.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) submit BRS on a monthly basis per bank account in accordance with Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and Chapter 21 of the GAM for LGU, Volume I; (ii) observe proper accounting treatment in recognizing stale checks; and (iii) prepare an adjusting entry for the Cash-in-Bank Account and recognize back the Accounts Payable related to the stale checks in the aggregate amount of ₱31,363.44; and (b) Municipal Treasurer monitor the checks drawn against the bank accounts of the Municipality and observe sending written notice to the payees informing them of the existence of a check at least one month before they become stale.

We reiterate our prior year’s audit recommendation and the LCE agreed to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to (i) recognize only the final and executory audit disallowances and charges in the account, Receivables – Disallowances/Charges; (ii) update the records on the account Receivables – Disallowances/Charges and reconcile with the Audit Team’s records in order to make the necessary adjustment in the Agency’s books; (iii) continue identifying the reconciling items between the reciprocal accounts Due from/to Other Funds and effect the adjustments in the accounting records, as necessary; and (iv) improve the monitoring of these reciprocal accounts by ensuring that entries made/posted from one account have a parallel recognition/posting to the other.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Accounting Office and the Supply Officer, as the case may be, to: (i) maintain the Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards for all LGU inventories and observe the periodic reconciliation of the two records to monitor agency assets properly; (ii) conduct a complete semi-annual physical count of inventories and submit the corresponding RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year in accordance with Section 124 of the MNGAs for LGUs; (iii) maintain a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to serve as a basis for preparing journal entries to record the supplies and materials issued; (iv) maintain a WMR to establish transparency regarding the legitimacy of replacements and disposal of waste materials; and (v) conform with the implementing guidelines of COA Circular 2022-004 regarding the issuance of ICS.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Inventory Committee, Accounting Office and the Property Officer, as the case may be, to: (i) conduct a complete annual physical count of PPEs and come up with the RPCPPE in accordance with Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs; (ii) conduct an investigation on the missing PPEs and if warranted establish who are the accountable officers for their loss by collating all the Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) on file; (iii) maintain the Property Cards and PPE Ledger Cards for all LGUs properties and observe the periodic reconciliation of the two records to monitor agency’s assets properly; and (iv) maintain an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to properly document and track the unserviceable assets of the Municipality.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with a certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and (ii) coordinate with the Municipal Assessors Office in updating the master list of taxpayers to exclude properties which are exempted from the payment of RPT.

We reiterate our prior year’s audit recommendation and LCE agreed to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain SLs per creditor as necessary to support the balances presented in the GL and financial statements; and (ii) exhaust all remedies to gather the details and supporting documents needed for the identification of the amounts of dormant and long-outstanding payables to facilitate the reversion and/or write-off of such in accordance with the provisions of COA-DBM JC No. 1, s. 2021 and COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (i) instruct the process owners of the Local Roads Asset Management System based on COA Circular 2015-008 to be mindful of their duties laid down in the said Circular; (ii) require the Supply/Property Officer to come up with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road Network Property Card for all roads under the Agency’s jurisdiction; and (iii) require the Accounting Office to disclose information on Road Networks in the Notes to the FS in accordance with COA Circular 2015-008.

We recommended and the LCE concurred and Municipal Accountant to a) maintain the punctual submission of documents relating to CF; b) provide and clearly state in the DV the PPAs and the duration of implementation of the projects so that if the implementation of the project will extend to more than three months, additional amount may be granted only after liquidation of the previous cash advance; and c) employ a more efficient and timely release of cash advance for CF so as not to derail the prompt accomplishment and implementation of projects under the said fund.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continue upholding its mandate in promoting local development by appropriating 20% of its annual NTA pursuant to the provisions of the LGC and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and JMC No. 01 dated November 04, 2020; and (b) Engineering Office and MPDO ensure timely delivery of the projects for CY 2023 and for every budget year, taking into account the legal rudiments of every project; (c) the Local Development Council (LDC), (i) continue to give priority to projects focusing on inclusive growth and sustainable development of communities at the barangay level; and (ii) maintain the proactive involvement of the barangays thru the LDC in identifying socio-economic projects to be implemented out of the DF; (d) Budget Officer and the BAC to ensure the availability of fund specifically intended for a particular PPA before awarding contracts; and (e) Budget Officer to secure the certificate of availability of fund from the barangays with their counterpart amounts and verify whether the fund transferred to the Municipality by the Barangays come from the latter’s DF or as budgeted in their annual budget to avoid legal issues.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) BAC and TWG to (i) submit the lacking documents as enumerated in the discussion; (ii) prepare the BAC resolution declaring Single Calculated and Responsive Bidder (SCRB) if there is only one responsive bidder as provided by Section 36 of IRR of R.A. 9184; (iii) ensure that all required postings in PhilGEPS as mentioned in the Revised IRR are complied within the period prescribed. Moreover, attach a printed copy of all postings to the related disbursement vouchers; (iv) conduct the post-qualification exercise through significant verification based on the documents under examination so as to establish the integrity of documents and to detect misrepresentation; and (b) instruct the Accounting Office to check and ensure the completeness of documents and exert the proper diligence to discern that the documents submitted by the contractors are legitimate before effecting payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Management (a) direct the MSWMB to (i) to prioritize the review/update of the SWMP to ensure its sustainability, viability and effectiveness; (ii) prepare Long-term Action Plan and Annual Work Plan and Budget showing the SWM activities to be implemented and the financial counterpart for each SWM activity; and (iii) continue to improve the work of the monitoring and evaluation team and require them to prepare Progress Reports in line with the Annual Work Plan and the SWM Plan; (b) resume the operation of the MRF, particularly the organic fertilizer and bricks production, as soon as possible; and (c) Municipal Budget Officer to further classify/break-down the SWM Budget into different accounts based on the nature of the goods/services to be procured for each program, project and activity (PPA).

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the: a) Municipal Accountant to continue to (i) make necessary corrections/adjustments in affected inventory and expense accounts; (ii) observe the perpetual method in recognizing inventory and avoid recording outright expense when accounting for inventoriable goods and DRRM supplies; (iii) continue to utilize the LDRRMF only for expenses projects that are directly related to disaster risk reduction and preparation/prevention in accordance with NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013; and (iv) discuss and present the minimum required disclosure in the Notes to the FS as specified in COA Circular No. 2014-002; and b) Supply Officer, in coordination with concerned offices, to maintain Stock Cards and prepare and submit the RIS, SSMI and Inventory List of Items for DRRM to the Municipal Accountant for recording and adjusting related expense and inventory accounts.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) members of the BAC/TWG to (i) adhere to the provisions of the Revised IRR as to the completeness of documents in executing government contracts from requisition, bidding up to award; and (ii) submit the lacking documents presented from the table; and (b) Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of documents before effecting payments.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continuously and constantly monitor, intensify and strategize collection efforts from taxpayers such as sending payment advisory or notice to taxpayers before the payment period or deadlines and the like; (b) instruct the Business Permit and Licensing Office (BPLO) and Municipal Treasurer’s Office to (i) include copy of Certified Income Tax Return in the list of required documents for the application for business permit, as required by the Municipal Revenue Code and (ii) request from the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) the list of all BIR registered business establishments in Mayantoc, Tarlac with their declared gross sales before the start of the licensing period; (c) direct the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to conduct the examination of books of accounts and pertinent records in accordance with Section 171 of the LGC, especially businesses/taxpayers who failed to submit their ITR; and (d) instruct the SB to follow up on the status of the Revised/Updated Revenue Code with the Sangguniang Panlalawigan.

We recommended and LCE agreed to (i) continue to remit promptly and intact the loan amortization and mandatory contributions to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth within the prescribed period; (ii) exert extra effort in gathering pertinent documents and analyze the subject accounts to establish the accuracy of the balances in the inter-agency payables; and (iii) remit all the due premiums immediately, when evidence warrants.

We recommended and the LCE concurred that the Municipal Accountant and other concerned personnel enforce the immediate settlement of disallowances, particularly those which have been rendered final and executory, in accordance with Sections 9.4 and 10.4, respectively, of the 2009 RRSA.

1.5 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed that the (a)
Municipal Accountant (i) submit BRS on a monthly basis per bank account in
accordance with Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and
Chapter 21 of the GAM for LGU, Volume I; (ii) observe proper accounting treatment
in recognizing stale checks; and (iii) prepare an adjusting entry for the Cash-in-Bank

update the records on the account Receivables – Disallowances/Charges and reconcile
with the Audit Team’s records in order to make the necessary adjustment in the

accounts Due from/to Other Funds and effect the adjustments in the accounting
records, as necessary; and (iv) improve the monitoring of these reciprocal accounts
by ensuring that entries made/posted from one account have a parallel
recognition/posting to the other.

the Supply Officer, as the case may be, to: (i) maintain the Stock Cards and Supplies
Ledger Cards for all LGU inventories and observe the periodic reconciliation of the

31 and January 31 of each year in accordance with Section 124 of the MNGAs for
LGUs; (iii) maintain a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to serve

(iv) maintain a WMR to establish transparency regarding the legitimacy of

5.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed that Management instruct the
Municipal Treasurer to (i) furnish the Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the
year with a certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and
collectible for the year; and (ii) coordinate with the Municipal Assessors Office in
updating the master list of taxpayers to exclude properties which are exempted from
the payment of RPT.

6.11 We reiterate our prior year’s audit recommendation and LCE agreed to
enjoin the Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain SLs per creditor as
necessary to support the balances presented in the GL and financial statements; and
(ii) exhaust all remedies to gather the details and supporting documents needed for
the identification of the amounts of dormant and long-outstanding payables to
facilitate the reversion and/or write-off of such in accordance with the provisions of
COA-DBM JC No. 1, s. 2021 and COA Circular No. 2016-005.

Officer to come up with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road

the Accounting Office to disclose information on Road Networks in the Notes to the
FS in accordance with COA Circular 2015-008.

8.9 We recommended and the LCE concurred and Municipal Accountant to a)
maintain the punctual submission of documents relating to CF; b) provide and clearly
state in the DV the PPAs and the duration of implementation of the projects so that
if the implementation of the project will extend to more than three months, additional
amount may be granted only after liquidation of the previous cash advance; and c)
employ a more efficient and timely release of cash advance for CF so as not to derail

9.15 We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continue upholding its mandate
in promoting local development by appropriating 20% of its annual NTA pursuant
to the provisions of the LGC and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22,

Solid Waste Management Plan | Solid Waste Management Plan | Solid Waste Management Plan
Component | Description | Remarks
City and Municipal Profile | Includes the estimated population of each barangay under the Municipality for a 10-year period, illustration/maps of the municipality indicating commercial and industrial centers, and agricultural area, as well as dump sites, landfills and other solid waste facilities. | The Municipal SWM plan provided the estimated population of the 24 barangays under its jurisdiction. However, maps that were attached in the plan were not clear and do not properly depict the locations of residential and industrial areas of the municipality.
Waste Characterization | It should include the composition of the solid waste generated within the jurisdiction of the LGU. The constituent materials shall be identified by volume, percentage in weight or its volumetric equivalent, material type and source of generation. | Included in the Municipal SWMP was the projected total wastes disposed by the municipality, arranged by waste type (biodegradable, recyclable, residual, and special waste), and by producer (household, non-household). However, upon further inquiry, we found out that the data included in the 2014 10-year SWM plan was gathered through Waste Analysis Characterization Study (WACS) last 2006, and the data was only subsequently rolled-over in the 2014 Municipal SMWP. Thus, the data may already be unreliable and may not represent the current composition of wastes found within the Municipality because of the substantial length of time from the past study to the current period.
Collection and Transfer | The plan shall define the coverage of the solid waste collection area in every barangay, and will be subject to factors such as the availability of properly designed receptacles for waste storage, waste segregation, hauling and transfer from collection point to processing sites, issuance and enforcement of ordinances to effectively implement a collection system and a provision of properly trained officers and workers to handle solid waste disposal.  The plan shall also define and specify the methods and systems for the transfer of solid waste from specific collection points to solid waste management facilities | We noted through inquiry that the collection coverage of the Municipality has not substantially increased as it currently collects wastes from only 5 of the 24 barangays under its jurisdiction. There is also no discussion on the collection schedule and the orderly and systematic collection of wastes within the coverage area (includes Brgys. PoblacionNorte, Poblacion Sur, San Bartolome, Ambalingit and Rotrottooc).
d) Processing | The plan shall define the methods and facilities required to process the solid waste, including the use of treatment facilities for composting, recycling, conversion and other waste processing system. | The SWM plan mentioned the MRF located in Brgys. Poblacion Norte and Ambalingit. However, according to the MENRO Officer, the facility was already abandoned and is not operational anymore. We also noted that the Municipality directly transports all wastes transported to Calangitan, Capas after the closure of the open pit dumpsite in Brgy. Gayong-gayong.
Source reduction, recycling and composting | The plan shall have a program and implementation schedule which shows the methods by which the LGU will reduce the amount of solid waste disposed of through recycling, composting and other waste reduction projects. | The schedule of projected total residual wastes to be disposed as stated in the SWM plan shows a decreasing amount to be disposed of per year, from 2,590.49 metric tons in 2014 to only 808 metric tons in 2023. However, we observe that the Municipality currently has no waste reduction programs in place and the Municipality’s constituents do not have incentive to make an effort and reduce their current level of waste production, thus the projection of waste reduction may not be feasible. Also, the lack of operational recycling programs and composting facilities of the Municipality is a deterrent in its goal of waste reduction.
Solid Waste Facility Capacity and Final Disposal | The plan shall identify existing and proposed disposal sites and waste management facilities in the city or municipality or in other areas. | It was stated in the plan that the open pit dumpsite was previously the repository of the disposed wastes of the Municipality. However, as discussed in the SWMB meeting last June 2, 2017, the open-pit dumpsite in Gayong-gayong was already ordered for closure by the Municipality upon the recommendation of the EMB. Upon inspection of the site, walls and fences were already built and the dumpsite was already graded with soil. As an alternative, the Municipality entered into contract with Metro Clark Waste Management Corporation last February 1, 2017. It stipulates that the Municipality has an agreement to dump their collected wastes in its sanitary landfill located in Calangitan, Capas. As of this date, the Municipality transports its wastes daily in the said landfill.
Education and Public Information | The component shall describe how the LGU will educate the citizens about source reduction, recycling and composting programs. | It was stated in the plan that house to house campaign at the Barangay level and trainings in ESWM by barangay and purok officials will be held. The MENRO officer attested to the conduct of campaigns with various barangays and schools but no proof has been presented to the audit team regarding the seminars/campaigns conducted and initiated by the Municipality.
Special Waste | It shall include existing waste handling and disposal practices for special waste or hazardous household wastes | It was stated in the SWM plan that no separate collection was done for special wastes. These wastes are collected together with regular household wastes, contrary to the recommended handling of special wastes per Section 17(j) of RA No. 9003 which states that the Municipality shall identify proposed programs to ensure the proper handling, re-use and long-term disposal of special wastes.
